meta.title: The Metrics Gap: Uncovering Net-New Metrics in Solana's Ledger

transcript: Hi everyone, my name is Alejandra Martinez and I'm an investor at Foundation Capital, an early-stage VC fund in Silicon Valley. We've been proud investors in the Solana ecosystem since 2018 and are excited to join you on stage today. Today we'll be talking about how blockchain data needs to evolve with its context and what we can learn from metrics and their evolution in the early days of the internet. So current consensus is that the most important metric in crypto is the one currently going up. It's only natural. We're still in the early stage of crypto and we're still largely momentum driven. And these big growing numbers help us share the massive opportunity that we see in front of us. We've all been through hoops to find these numbers, whether it is the more than 75 developers still building on Solana or the growing number of transactions. The challenge is that a large number isn't necessarily the one that is going to take us to scale. All new industries start with what sound like slightly ridiculous metrics before we find the way to metrics that matter. When my fund was started in 1995, my partners at the time were constantly hearing metrics like the number of eyeballs as a sign of impending success. Over the next five years, the NASDAQ grew 500%. But eyeballs measurement was inconsistent, unverifiable, and not tied to the success of a business model. The infrastructure to convert eyeballs into sales was still very nascent. And it really did not make up for the increasing costs in the industry at the time. The market crashed, destroying 5 trillions of value. That is more than the entire crypto economy today. You fast forward to the marketplace era, and outliers like Facebook, Uber, and Airbnb couldn't use old metrics like revenue and EBITDA to demonstrate their potential early on. So these outliers surfaced what were net-new metrics at the time to demonstrate their network effect that they were building. They initially used metrics like registered users, interactions a day, and GMV equivalents to demonstrate their early monetization potential. And as their data troves grew, they navigated towards metrics that were more around user behavior, retention, and CAC. These metrics became highly indicative of the success of these companies later on and laid the groundwork for stuff like A/B testing, more strategic data-driven decisions, and early valuations. Meaningful metrics tend to precede really good reporting. And the tech metrics that came from these companies got rigorous enough to make their way into regulated financial documents. Early 10-K reports had surfaced from the industrial era and were great for companies like a widgets business, but weren't really great for a company like a software company or a marketplace that has delayed revenues. A dual narrative emerged with GAAP financials offering comparability and compliance, but non-GAAP metrics being used to determine growth and valuations. External stakeholders began valuing companies on average revenue per users and contracted ARR for the first time. And by 2015, almost 90% of companies reported non-GAAP metrics. A model for valuing companies on activity before revenue and EBITDA had emerged, but it took a while to get there. In crypto, we're still in our early internet era for metrics. The same gameable metrics are applied evenly across many protocols. Lack of standardization is leading to misinformation and nerd sniping on Twitter over definitions. And our growth story is often left to interpretation on Crypto Twitter and external dashboards. As an investor, it's a little bit frustrating sometimes to get an external dashboard as our only view into a company's data. Not because these other dashboards aren't great, but it doesn't really give us a sense for how the founder or the founding team is thinking about their business. Structural differences with Web2 are demanding new frameworks for how we think about data. In closed systems, a company is creating their data and is selectively telling us what they want us to hear. But this data is not updated in real time, and we don't have any real-time feedback from what is happening in the rest of those ecosystems. In crypto, finding metrics and data is a lot harder. No one controls the full picture. Wallets aren't necessarily unique users. And the anonymity of the data is obscuring a lot of the insight. But the open architecture means we can query a lot more information. We get updates in real time, and state changes aren't isolated. Just within our portfolio, a buyback on Ore goes to Orca's AMM, is processed through Helius, and then bundled in Jito. That means that a ton of new data is packed in a matter of just seconds. But there's a limited amount of people who can read the chain today. And for most, searching for that data is almost like looking at Neo... at the Matrix before Neo's vision. But with some work, we move from seeing raw numbers to seeing people, relationships, and meaning. And we can uncover whether we're giving away free candy that nobody likes or our biggest power user has started to see somebody else. Solana has processed over 450 billion transactions since the genesis block. And that's more than 400 terabytes of data we can sift through to analyze. But today, our transactions mostly look like this on explorers. And our holders look like this. Our top holders, we often don't know if top holder number two is the same as top holder number eight. But we have the tools to dissect this data with forensic rigor. We can map and tag wallets to understand the meaning of the movements in those accounts. We can look through chain history and look at individual transaction data or decide whether we need to zoom out and start getting meaning from aggregating data across the chain. There is a massive opportunity to do transaction analysis at scale to the likes of Chainalysis or TRM, but for user analysis. And advancements in AI are giving us the ability to read on-chain data a lot more effectively. It would be impossible to manually interpret the quantity of data that is generated in a high throughput chain manually. And now we have tools like Orb, a block explorer from our portfolio company Helius, where one can input a transaction and get the meaning of that transaction in natural language. Meaning even your non-technical team members can start reading into some of the blockchain data. We're about to unlock an explosion of new data standards, and we've already started to see it via updates in analytics tools. We have a very solid start at the base layer, with Solana's metrics moving up the stack as the network evolved. When we initially invested in Solana in 2018, it was all about TPS. Then we moved on to looking at reliability. And once performance issues were figured out, we moved on to looking at revenue, both at the protocol and at the app layer. What determined the network's opportunity early on was its ability to scale. But what is determining the network's opportunity now is telling others, 'We have built a platform here where others are making money, and so can you.' This curated metrics allowed Solana to natively tell the story of the chain. But what works for the base layer won't necessarily work for applications. Facebook and Uber didn't try to retrofit old metrics to apply to their own business models, and neither should new protocols on Solana. Our new business models are surfacing net-new metrics. And so if we look at some examples, our portfolio company DoubleZero, which started on Solana, was measuring its adoption as a percentage of stake on the chain. But now, as its go-to-market motion expands beyond Solana, they're looking at Total Connected Value as a measure of adoption. Let's take Bonk as another example. This beloved native meme was all about price early on. We were looking at metrics like partnerships and burn to understand how the price was gonna move. But now that it's turned into an operating company, different metrics matter. And indulge me for a second at what a Solana-native metric could look like. Bonk is connected to over 350 other protocols on Solana. So it's all about composability. Some of these metrics could look like ecosystem engagement depth. Or we could also be looking at fees over programmatic incentives that are updated as we get more information on the chain. Ultimately, net-new metrics showcase how companies on Solana are gonna be built differently. And we expect to see an explosion of new data standards to surface very soon. With more ledger data than many other chain, Solana is the place where we expect net-new metrics to emerge. We'll be rewarded for better storytelling, and we'll build better products as a result. We'll see more stuff like A/B testing and better strategic decisions being made. This is beginning to happen natively with the growing sophistication of metrics and analytics tools. So the question is, are you going to tell your story or are you going to let others tell it for you? We're actively investing in the ecosystem and are really excited about the early-stage companies building in the space. So if you want to chat, feel free to reach out to me directly. Thank you for the time.

editorial.headline: Foundation Capital: Net-New Metrics Will Define Solana's Winners
editorial.standfirst: Current crypto metrics are akin to dot-com "eyeballs"—inconsistent and gameable. Foundation Capital argues that institutional maturity requires "net-new" standards focusing on composability, retention, and total connected value rather than raw throughput.
key_takeaways: **Foundation Capital** warns that generic metrics like TPS and active wallets are failing to capture true protocol value, drawing parallels to the "eyeballs" metric that masked the lack of business models before the 2000 dot-com crash.
Portfolio company **DoubleZero** has shifted its primary KPI from "percentage of stake" to **"Total Connected Value"** to better represent its go-to-market efficiency and cross-chain expansion.

AI-driven tools like **Helius's Orb** are now enabling natural language querying of Solana's 400TB ledger, allowing investors to track "ecosystem engagement depth" and distinguish genuine power users from sybil attackers.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The industry is currently stuck in a "metrics gap" reminiscent of the 1999 dot-com bubble, where vanity metrics like "eyeballs" masked a lack of viable business models. **Foundation Capital**, an early backer of **Solana**, argues that the current reliance on momentum-driven numbers—such as raw transaction counts and active wallets—is insufficient for the next phase of institutional adoption. Just as SaaS companies eventually standardized around non-GAAP metrics like ARR and CAC to justify valuations, crypto protocols must evolve beyond generic dashboards to demonstrate genuine network effects and user behavior. The market crash of 2000 destroyed $5 trillion in value because infrastructure didn't match the hype; avoiding a similar fate in crypto requires data standards that are verifiable and tied to revenue.

The sheer volume of data on **Solana**—over 450 billion transactions and 400 terabytes of history—presents a unique arbitrage opportunity for investors willing to look deeper. Unlike the closed data silos of Web2, Solana's open architecture allows for real-time forensic analysis. However, this data has historically been illegible to non-technical stakeholders. The emergence of AI-powered explorers like **Helius's Orb** is changing this dynamic, converting raw hash data into natural language insights. This shift allows analysts to map wallet relationships and distinguish between "free candy" sybil attackers and genuine power users, moving from raw numbers to a forensic understanding of retention.

Specific protocols are already pioneering these "net-new" metrics to tell a more compelling growth story. **DoubleZero**, initially focused on staking percentages, now tracks **Total Connected Value** to measure its go-to-market efficiency. Similarly, **Bonk** has transitioned from a meme coin narrative centered on burn rates to an operating company model, where success is measured by **ecosystem engagement depth** across its 350+ integrations. For institutional allocators, the alpha lies in identifying teams that optimize for these sophisticated, composability-focused KPIs rather than vanity metrics. As **Foundation Capital** notes, meaningful metrics tend to precede rigorous reporting, and the protocols that define these standards today will likely command the premium valuations of tomorrow.
